meta.title: Project | kalandra.tech — Live-Built Showcase
meta.description: Live-built showcase documenting the architecture, roadmap, and technical decisions behind kalandra.tech — from static site to full-stack SaaS.

hero.description: This page is an evolving experiment in digital architecture. It serves as a live-built showcase of my skills where every iteration is documented, from initial wireframes to advanced features.

items.title: Personal Showcase
items.description: Present myself to potential employers and recruiters.

items.title: Skills Demonstration
items.description: Document the building process as part of the showcase — architecture, decisions, and progress.

items.title: Technology Playground
items.description: Experiment with new technologies in a real, deployed project.

nfrItems.label: Automation
nfrItems.description: Minimal manual overhead. Infrastructure as code or GitHub Actions for everything.

nfrItems.label: Reliability
nfrItems.description: The site must be up reliably.

nfrItems.label: Cost
nfrItems.description: As cheap as possible — preferably free. Backend hosting: Oracle Cloud Always Free (fallback: Hetzner VPS ~€4/month).

decisions.title: Architecture Decisions
decisions.subtitle: Key technical decisions documented as Architecture Decision Records.

adrs.title: Backend Hosting → Oracle Cloud
adrs.summary: Oracle Cloud Always Free ARM A1 instance, with Hetzner VPS as fallback.
adrs.context: Need a host that supports long-running processes (ASP.NET Core + Temporal background workers). Most free tiers are serverless/scale-to-zero and shut down the process when idle.
adrs.decision: Start with Oracle Cloud Always Free ARM A1 instance (4 OCPUs, 24 GB RAM, 200 GB storage). If Oracle proves unreliable, fall back to Hetzner VPS (~€4/month).
why: Free tier with generous specs (4 OCPUs, 24 GB RAM, 200 GB storage)
Supports long-running processes and Docker
Upgrading to PAYG (still free) disables idle reclamation

risks.details: Oracle rejects many signups based on region demand and payment method
risks.mitigation: Use real credit card, match billing address, pick less popular region

risks.details: Popular regions often have no A1 capacity available
risks.mitigation: Choose EU or less popular region at signup

risks.details: Instances with <20% CPU at P95 over 7 days are stopped after 1 week notice
risks.mitigation: Real workloads stay above threshold; PAYG disables this entirely

risks.details: Rare reports of accounts suspended without clear reason
risks.mitigation: Keep PAYG enabled; maintain real usage

setupNotes: Use Ubuntu 24.04 Minimal aarch64 (not Oracle Linux) for straightforward Docker support
Set a large boot volume (~150 GB) at creation to avoid iSCSI block volume complexity
Open ports in both OCI security list and OS firewall

alternatives.reason: Reliable fallback. Full control, predictable. Will switch here if Oracle causes trouble.
60 min CPU/day, no always-on — app sleeps after inactivity.
Serverless — no persistent background process for Temporal workers.
Spins down after 15 min inactivity. Background workers are paid only.
Free tier has gotten stingier; likely ends up costing a few dollars anyway.
Free tier is generous, but self-hosting Temporal would be awkward.

adrs.title: Frontend Framework → Astro SSG with Vue Islands
adrs.summary: Astro in SSG mode with Vue components hydrated only where needed.
adrs.context: Need a frontend framework for a mostly-static site hosted on Cloudflare Pages. Requirements: Tailwind CSS, i18n (Czech/English), progressive interactivity, minimal JS shipped to users.
adrs.decision: Astro in static site generation (SSG) mode. Interactive components built as Vue islands, hydrated only where needed. No Cloudflare Workers — Pages serves plain HTML/CSS/JS from CDN.
why: Ships zero JS by default — only interactive islands include client-side code
Built-in i18n routing and locale detection
Cloudflare acquired Astro (Jan 2026) — best-in-class Pages integration
.astro template syntax feels similar to HTML/Vue — low learning curve
Vue skills transfer directly to a future Vue/Nuxt project if heavier UI logic is needed

alternatives.reason: Still needs a Tailwind build step. Would have to manually build routing, i18n, and component system.
Ships ~50-80 KB Vue runtime to every page even when content is static.
Ships ~80-120 KB React runtime. JS-first authoring. Next.js is Vercel-aligned, not Cloudflare-aligned.
Good framework, but i18n is not built-in. More geared toward interactive apps.
SSR model runs server-side functions — effectively a second backend. Goal is a thin static client with a single ASP.NET Core backend.

adrs.title: Event Sourcing → Marten
adrs.summary: Marten event sourcing for job offer lifecycle tracking on PostgreSQL.
adrs.context: Job offers have a lifecycle (Submitted → InReview → Accepted/Declined/Cancelled) where tracking the full history of changes is valuable for both the user and admin.
adrs.decision: Use Marten with event sourcing for job offer state management.
why: Events provide a natural audit trail — every status change is captured immutably
The activity log (event stream) is a first-class feature, not a bolted-on audit table
Inline snapshot projections maintain read models with zero extra code
Uses PostgreSQL directly — same database, no additional infrastructure
The Apply() method pattern makes state transitions explicit and testable

adrs.title: Admin Role → JWT Claims
adrs.summary: Roles stored in Supabase app_metadata, extracted from JWT on each request.
adrs.context: The app needs role-based authorization (regular users vs admin) without maintaining a separate roles table in the backend.
adrs.decision: Roles are stored as an array in Supabase app_metadata (e.g., "roles": ["admin"]). The backend extracts roles from the JWT and maps each to a standard .NET role claim.
why: No server-side user ID list or database roles table needed — roles travel with the token
Standard .NET authorization infrastructure (RequireRole) works out of the box
Adding new roles = updating app_metadata in Supabase, no backend changes

adrs.title: Auth → Supabase (Local + Production)
adrs.summary: JWT validation via JWKS, with symmetric secret fallback for tests and local dev.
adrs.context: The backend needs to validate Supabase JWTs, and local development needs to work without an internet connection or external Supabase project.
adrs.decision: The backend validates JWT signatures via JWKS (fetching public keys from Supabase's OpenID Connect endpoint). It never calls the Supabase API directly. A symmetric secret fallback exists for tests and local Supabase.
why: Local dev runs a full Supabase instance in Docker — no external dependencies
Production uses Supabase Cloud with Google OAuth + email/password
E2E tests use local Supabase with programmatic user creation
Backend integration tests generate JWTs with a known test secret via Testcontainers

adrs.title: Code Organization → Vertical Slices
adrs.summary: Code organized by feature, not by technical layer.
adrs.context: Need a backend code organization strategy that keeps related code together and avoids the "change one feature, touch five layers" problem.
adrs.decision: Code is organized by feature (e.g., Features/JobOffers/) rather than by technical layer. Each feature folder contains its controller, events, DTOs, and handlers.
why: Adding a new feature = adding a new folder with all its files, no changes to shared layers
Each feature is self-contained — easy to understand, review, and test in isolation
Avoids the typical layered architecture problem where a single change touches Controllers/, Services/, Models/, DTOs/, etc.

adrs.title: Testing Strategy
adrs.summary: Integration tests with real databases, Playwright for UI and E2E, no mocks.
adrs.context: Need a testing approach that provides high confidence with minimal mocking, covering the full stack from API endpoints to database.
adrs.decision: Multi-layer testing with real infrastructure at every level.
why: Backend integration tests: xUnit + Testcontainers — real PostgreSQL, full API via WebApplicationFactory, generated JWTs, no mocks
Frontend page tests: Playwright — builds the static site, verifies rendering, navigation, i18n, and dark mode
E2E smoke tests: Playwright against full stack (frontend + backend + DB)
CI/CD: Backend and frontend tests run in separate pipelines, both block deployment on failure

items.title: Temporal + Supabase Database
items.description: Temporal requires its own database. Pointing it at the Supabase PostgreSQL might eat into the free tier's connection, storage, and egress limits.
options: Use Supabase PostgreSQL for Temporal — monitor quota usage
Run a separate SQLite or PostgreSQL instance on the VPS for Temporal
Fall back to Azure Queue Storage if Temporal overhead is too high

versions.title: Emails, Slack & Observability
versions.description: Email confirmations and Slack notifications on job offer submissions and status changes. Full observability stack: Sentry for error tracking, PostHog for product analytics, BetterStack for logging and uptime.

versions.title: Background Tasks
versions.description: Move emails and notifications into background processing. Self-hosted Temporal on the backend VPS with retry semantics. Fallback: Azure Queue Storage.

versions.title: Pay to Win (Stripe)
versions.description: Monetize job offer submissions via Stripe with tiered pricing.
